collection_policy.objective: Upgrade the Mark Twain persona line from a minimal demo pack to a catalog-scale source inventory that can seed temporal graph ingestion, provenance tracking, and persona evidence slicing.
selection_rules: Prioritize public-domain primary texts for works, essays, speeches, autobiography materials, and letters.
Use metadata-only or authority references for biography and scholarly framing when full-text redistribution is not clearly open.
Favor sources that expose distinct voice phases such as early journalism, river years, lecture years, anti-imperialist turn, and late dictation/autobiography years.
Record only metadata and short analytical summaries in this catalog; do not reproduce long copyrighted passages.
normalization_notes: This catalog is the master inventory. Individual source JSON records can be derived from these entries later.

The voice_sources bucket follows the merged scope requested by the project: interviews, quotations, speeches, essays, letters, and autobiography fragments may all count if they materially expose Twain's reasoning rhythm or speaking persona.

works.title: The Adventures of Tom Sawyer, Complete
works.why_it_matters: Essential for Twain's boyhood lens, comic pacing, vernacular warmth, and his habit of mixing play with moral danger.

works.title: Adventures of Huckleberry Finn
works.why_it_matters: Critical for persona construction because it captures Twain's strongest vernacular narration, irony toward social hypocrisy, and moral testing through voice.

works.title: The Innocents Abroad
works.why_it_matters: Provides the early travel-satire persona, especially Twain's method of puncturing prestige, tourism cliche, and inherited cultural reverence.

works.title: Roughing It
works.why_it_matters: Useful for the frontier-adventure phase, revealing Twain's self-mythologizing humor, exaggeration mechanics, and observational storytelling.

works.title: Life on the Mississippi
works.why_it_matters: Anchors the river-pilot identity and explains the practical, technical, memory-rich layer behind Twain's later wit and authority.

works.title: Following the Equator: A Journey Around the World
works.why_it_matters: Important for the later global and anti-imperial phase, where Twain's social criticism becomes more weary, sharp, and morally explicit.

works.title: A Tramp Abroad
works.why_it_matters: Strengthens the European travel-comedy layer and Twain's technique of staging naive American perception against older cultural systems.

works.title: The Prince and the Pauper
works.why_it_matters: Shows Twain's interest in class, injustice, and role reversal, which is useful for extracting his ethical framing beyond pure humor.

works.title: A Connecticut Yankee in King Arthur's Court
works.why_it_matters: Central for Twain's anti-romantic and anti-feudal satire, and for his tendency to test technology, progress, and power against moral consequences.

works.title: The Tragedy of Pudd'nhead Wilson
works.why_it_matters: Useful for race, identity, law, and fate themes, and for building a darker Twain stance layer with stronger social critique.

works.title: The American Claimant
works.why_it_matters: Helps represent Twain's comedy of status reversal and his running comparison between democracy, aristocracy, and performance.

works.title: The Gilded Age: A Tale of Today
works.why_it_matters: Important for political corruption, speculative greed, and Twain's satirical treatment of public life and opportunism.

voice_sources.title: What Is Man? and Other Essays
voice_sources.why_it_matters: A core voice source for deterministic skepticism, argument-by-dialogue, and Twain's habit of exposing moral certainty as self-flattering illusion.

voice_sources.title: How to Tell a Story, and Other Essays
voice_sources.why_it_matters: Directly useful for modeling Twain's performance theory of humor, especially timing, indirection, deadpan buildup, and anti-overexplaining.

voice_sources.title: Mark Twain's Speeches
voice_sources.why_it_matters: This is the strongest overtly spoken-voice source for the public lecturer persona, including comic openings, social commentary, and audience-aware rhythm.

voice_sources.title: Chapters from My Autobiography
voice_sources.why_it_matters: Captures the late dictation mode: digressive, intimate, self-correcting, memory-driven, and ideal for temporal persona modeling.

voice_sources.title: Mark Twain's Burlesque Autobiography
voice_sources.why_it_matters: Useful for distinguishing Twain's mock-autobiographical voice from later reflective autobiography, especially when the persona should be more playful and self-parodic.

voice_sources.title: Editorial Wild Oats
voice_sources.why_it_matters: Important for the journalism layer and for early sharp-edged comic prose that later matures into a broader narrative persona.

voice_sources.title: Is Shakespeare Dead?
voice_sources.why_it_matters: A compact specimen of late-period argumentative Twain: skeptical, performative, anecdotal, and happy to needle prestige institutions.

voice_sources.title: Mark Twain's Letters — Volume 1 (1853-1866)
voice_sources.why_it_matters: Covers the formative years and helps ground the young Twain voice before literary celebrity stabilized his public persona.

voice_sources.title: Mark Twain's Letters — Volume 2 (1867-1875)
voice_sources.why_it_matters: Important for the breakthrough travel-and-lecture period, showing how Twain's published wit relates to private strategy and self-positioning.

voice_sources.title: Mark Twain's Letters — Volume 3 (1876-1885)
voice_sources.why_it_matters: Useful for tracing the mid-career household, publishing, and friendship network around the period of his most canonical fiction.

voice_sources.title: Mark Twain's Letters — Volume 4 (1886-1900)
voice_sources.why_it_matters: Important for financial crisis, travel, publishing pressure, and the widening gap between public wit and private strain.

voice_sources.title: Mark Twain's Letters — Volume 5 (1901-1906)
voice_sources.why_it_matters: Key for anti-imperial politics, late grief, and the sharper moral edge that should show up in a lead-reader persona.

voice_sources.title: Mark Twain's Letters — Volume 6 (1907-1910)
voice_sources.why_it_matters: Essential for the final-year register: public honors, mortality, fatigue, wit under sorrow, and late philosophical compression.

voice_sources.title: The Letters of Mark Twain
voice_sources.why_it_matters: Authoritative scholarly index for 12,000 plus letters, useful for later date-normalized edge construction and addressee-level graph expansion.

voice_sources.title: Autobiography of Mark Twain
voice_sources.why_it_matters: Provides authoritative chronology and access structure for dictations and early attempts, which is valuable when segmenting late-voice evidence by year.

biography_and_critical.title: Mark Twain: A Biography. Complete
biography_and_critical.why_it_matters: The core public-domain biographical spine for connecting life events, travel phases, business crises, and family losses to persona shifts.

biography_and_critical.title: Mark Twain
biography_and_critical.why_it_matters: Useful as a modern authority checkpoint for stable fact-layer claims and high-level summaries that should not depend on a single older biography.

biography_and_critical.title: Stories about Mark Twain: Topics in Chronicling America
biography_and_critical.why_it_matters: Useful for expanding into period newspaper evidence, reception history, and event-level temporal anchors around Twain's public presence.

notes: This catalog intentionally overbuilds the requested minimum so later filtering can keep only the highest-confidence entries without falling below threshold.

The works bucket emphasizes fiction and travel writing because those texts reveal sustained narrative habits; the voice_sources bucket emphasizes speech rhythm, argumentative habits, and autobiographical self-fashioning.

The current inventory is strong on public-domain books, speeches, essays, and letters, but still lighter on standalone newspaper interviews and single-speech event records that could sharpen date-level temporal edges.

A good next step after this catalog is to split each entry into individual source JSON files and then derive evidence slices with fields such as time_anchor_start, time_anchor_end, stance_tags, voice_mode, and candidate_graph_edges.
